assessment.scope: Core attendance check-in to points-feedback flow, with the adjacent main points-operation path.
assessment.overall_impression: The product has the bones of a fast classroom tool: current-session status, named student targets, explicit point deltas, and a batch points workflow are all present. The primary action is nevertheless visually subordinated to animated decoration and adjacent authoring tools, while the two flows use inconsistent feedback and accessibility patterns. The biggest opportunity is to make one-tap attendance feel immediate, honest, reversible, and legible across a classroom without turning routine feedback into a game-like interruption.
assessment.hierarchy: Weak. On attendance, the animated yellow quotation banner is the strongest object, ahead of the student grid and even the current session. On points, student selection and the reason toolbar are reasonably ordered, but settings, homework, running exercise, and history all remain on the same long page and compete for attention.
assessment.information_architecture: Attendance mixes three audiences and jobs in one surface: student self check-in, teacher notice authoring, and maintenance/settings. The points page likewise combines quick point adjustment with several specialized registers. The result is understandable to an experienced teacher but not task-focused.
assessment.emotional_fit: Mixed. Friendly language and visible growth cues fit the product, but continuous glow/breath/pulse animation, fireworks, bounce motion, emoji-heavy headings, and a celebration treatment for both punctual and late check-ins drift toward the low-trust, game-like tone the product explicitly rejects.
assessment.discoverability: The top navigation and attendance tabs use text labels and are easy to find. Today’s rules and current-session state help users understand availability. The horizontal reason rail hides its scrollbar and relies on drag behavior, point-selection cells are visually clickable but are non-semantic divs, and maintenance/authoring controls are interleaved with the student flow.
assessment.typography: The main app largely uses the established system sans family, but the attendance quotation introduces Georgia at a large, heavy, letter-spaced scale. This creates a second visual language and makes decorative copy dominate operational content. Several critical controls and metadata use 10–12px text, below the design system’s classroom-readability intent.
assessment.color: Semantic green/red/amber generally maps to success, penalty, and warning. However, the blue-to-indigo header gradient, saturated yellow quote, orange fireworks, and multi-colored feedback pills create an accent hierarchy that conflicts with the single-action-blue and restrained product rules. Disabled students use very pale gray with weak contrast.
assessment.accessibility: The attendance student targets are native buttons and include textual status, which is a good base. Major gaps remain: the points student grid is click-only div markup; the modal lacks dialog semantics, focus management, Escape behavior, and an accessible name on its icon-only close button; feedback has no live-region announcement; hover-only delete controls disappear from keyboard/touch users; focus treatment is inconsistent; and continuous animation has no prefers-reduced-motion fallback.
assessment.states: Current/late/non-period states and already-checked states exist, and out-of-session buttons are disabled. Missing or weak states include an in-flight state after tapping a student, visible retry/recovery for authentication or network failure, persistent confirmation, a clear explanation adjacent to disabled student buttons, and a post-points-change summary that includes the new balance.
assessment.copy: Most labels are concise and use classroom language. ‘打卡成功’ remains the heading even when the result is late and deducts points, which makes the emotional headline contradict the consequence. Native alerts such as ‘已打卡！’ and ‘打卡失败’ are abrupt, non-contextual, and do not consistently tell the user what to do next.
assessment.edge_cases: Repeated taps are server-guarded, but the UI has no per-student pending lock and the full-screen visual overlay uses pointer-events-none, so underlying actions remain hittable while the screen appears blocked. Session expiry or authentication failure can remove the user from context. Empty quotes can cause the daily-quote lookup to fail, long names have no explicit overflow treatment, and large classes expand the unfiltered grid indefinitely.

ai_slop_verdict.verdict: Mixed, with a clear slop pocket in attendance feedback rather than across the whole product.
evidence: The authenticated attendance screen uses a glowing, breathing yellow gradient quote in a serif display face above the actual task.
The feedback overlay stacks backdrop blur, rounded-2xl, shadow-2xl, a four-pixel orange border, fireworks, bounce/pop motion, pulse motion, and emoji for a routine action.
The main attendance container combines a full border with shadow-lg, reproducing the bordered ghost-card pattern rejected by the design guidance.
The surrounding dashboard and points path are more restrained and familiar, so the problem reads as inconsistent embellishment rather than an entirely synthetic interface.

visibility_of_system_status.key_issue: Current session and checked status are visible, but check-in has no pending state; success disappears after three seconds; errors fall back to alerts or loss of context.

match_between_system_and_real_world.key_issue: Classroom terms, time windows, names, and point deltas are natural, but ‘打卡成功’ conflicts with late/penalty outcomes.

user_control_and_freedom.key_issue: Points has cancel and recent-history undo, but attendance feedback cannot be dismissed or inspected later and offers no adjacent correction path.

consistency_and_standards.key_issue: Native alerts/confirms, the custom modal, click-only div selections, restrained work surfaces, and highly animated attendance feedback use different interaction vocabularies.

error_prevention.key_issue: Closed/already-completed buttons and server duplicate checks help, but no in-flight lock is shown and the pointer-transparent full-screen overlay can allow accidental underlying clicks.

recognition_rather_than_recall.key_issue: Named navigation, visible rules, student names, selected count, and reason values are strong; hidden horizontal overflow and unlabeled icon controls still demand inference.

flexibility_and_efficiency_of_use.key_issue: The points page supports filters, select-all, batches, and preset reasons, but there is no keyboard-efficient primary path, reason search, or scalable attendance filtering.

aesthetic_and_minimalist_design.key_issue: Core data remains readable, but decoration and unrelated tasks compete with the primary action, especially the animated quote and stacked attendance authoring/settings content.

help_users_recognize_diagnose_and_recove.key_issue: Errors are largely native alerts with little recovery guidance; the observed check-in attempt returned to login without an inline explanation or preserved task context.

help_and_documentation.key_issue: A few inline instructions exist, but there is no contextual help for disabled attendance, point semantics, session expiry, or correction after a mistaken tap.

heuristic_scores.rating: Acceptable — significant improvements needed before the flow fully supports trustworthy classroom use

single_focus.evidence: Teacher notices, the animated quote, rules, student grid, message board, settings, and global navigation compete on the check-in screen.

chunking.evidence: The attendance grid shows the entire class at once; the points surface exposes 10 reason choices and multiple full registration modules without stronger task-level chunking.

grouping.evidence: Notices, rules, student targets, messages, settings, filters, reasons, and history are visually grouped into recognizable regions.

visual_hierarchy.evidence: The decorative quote is the peak visual element while student selection and result confirmation should be primary.

one_thing_at_a_time.evidence: Both attendance and points keep unrelated secondary jobs visible while the user is completing a high-frequency primary action.

minimal_choices.evidence: Eight top-level navigation choices, 12 visible student targets, 10 point reasons, nine homework subjects, and six date choices exceed the four-item working-memory threshold at several decision points.

working_memory.evidence: Current session/rules remain visible in attendance, and the points path keeps the selected count and student preview available through confirmation.

progressive_disclosure.evidence: Settings are collapsed, but authoring, message, homework, running-exercise, and history modules are still presented alongside the main actions instead of behind task-level disclosure.

cognitive_load.rating: High cognitive load — critical flow-level distillation is needed

>4-option decision points.risk: Frequent tools and occasional/maintenance tools have equal top-level weight.
A full class is inherently large, but there is no group/search/seat-number aid for rapid targeting or very large rosters.
Hidden scrollbar and drag interaction make overflow and later reasons easy to miss.
Several independent decision sets are simultaneously visible below the already-complex quick-points flow.

emotional_journey.entry: The screen initially feels energetic and informative: the exact time, current period, and today’s rules reassure users that the system knows the classroom context.
emotional_journey.valley: Attention is pulled toward an animated quotation and teacher-authoring controls before the student’s actual action. Outside a session, pale disabled names communicate ‘not available’ but not the reason or next available time at the point of action.
emotional_journey.action_moment: Tapping a name is admirably direct, but the absence of a pending state makes the high-stakes moment feel uncertain and leaves room for repeat taps.
emotional_journey.peak: The intended feedback peak is visually loud and short-lived. A punctual student sees points and streak, but a late student receives the same celebratory ‘success’ framing before being told their streak ended and points were deducted.
emotional_journey.end: The overlay vanishes automatically after three seconds without leaving a persistent receipt, new balance, or clear correction link. In the isolated browser run, the check-in attempt instead returned to login with no visible explanation, an especially poor end state for a public classroom moment.
emotional_journey.peak_end_assessment: The flow over-invests in the peak animation and under-invests in the end-state receipt and recovery. A calmer, persistent, truthful result would create more trust and better reinforce growth.

strengths.title: Time and rule context reduce avoidable mistakes
strengths.detail: The header shows exact time/date, the current period, late status, and today’s windows; out-of-session and completed student buttons are disabled. This supports quick classroom judgment.

strengths.title: The data model supports meaningful feedback
strengths.detail: The UI already receives status, point delta, late-card use, and streak, so it can explain the consequence rather than showing a generic confirmation. Completed buttons also retain textual ‘已到/迟到’ status and time.

strengths.title: The adjacent points flow has a useful batch foundation
strengths.detail: Filters, select-all, selected count, reward/penalty segmentation, preset values, per-student adjustment, recent history, and undo create a plausible high-efficiency teacher workflow.

priority_issues.title: Primary flows are not keyboard- or assistive-technology complete
priority_issues.why_it_matters: A teacher using keyboard navigation cannot select students in the main points grid because each tile is a div with only onClick. Screen readers are not told which students are selected or when attendance succeeds. The modal lacks dialog semantics and focus containment, and icon-only/hover-only controls become undiscoverable.
priority_issues.fix: Render point student tiles as buttons with aria-pressed and visible focus; give the feedback receipt role=status or an aria-live region; implement a semantic dialog with aria-modal, labelled title, initial focus, focus trap, Escape/close behavior, and focus restoration; label every icon-only action; keep all primary targets at least 44 by 44 pixels; add prefers-reduced-motion fallbacks.

priority_issues.title: The attendance page has no single primary focus
priority_issues.why_it_matters: A student or teacher must visually pass an animated quote, notice authoring, rules, a full grid, a message composer, and settings. On a projector this makes the task feel like a portal rather than a fast check-in surface, increasing mis-taps and time at the front of class.
priority_issues.fix: Make current session plus student grid the first and dominant work area. Move quote to a quiet one-line secondary slot or remove it during active check-in; collapse teacher notice composition, message history, and maintenance into labelled teacher tools; keep settings outside the student-facing path. On points, use task tabs or progressive disclosure for quick points, homework, running exercise, and history rather than presenting all modules in one long page.

priority_issues.title: Feedback is visually loud but operationally fragile
priority_issues.why_it_matters: There is no per-student loading lock, the full-screen overlay visually blocks the page while pointer-events-none still allows underlying interaction, and the result disappears after three seconds. The browser run also observed a check-in tap returning to login without a visible reason or created record. These states undermine confidence in whether points changed.
priority_issues.fix: Show an immediate pending state on the tapped student and disable only relevant repeat actions. Replace the pointer-transparent spectacle with a persistent inline receipt anchored near the grid: student, status, exact delta, new balance, timestamp, and ‘查看/修正’ action. Preserve the attempted action across session expiry and show a plain-language re-login explanation with retry. Keep a short, non-blocking toast only as secondary reinforcement.

priority_issues.title: Routine attendance feedback breaks the product’s restrained visual language
priority_issues.why_it_matters: Continuous quote glow/breath/text-pulse, serif display type, gradient panels, fireworks, bounce/pop motion, large shadows, thick borders, and emoji-heavy success copy create a game-like island inside an otherwise familiar teacher tool. This is precisely the tonal direction the product anti-references reject.
priority_issues.fix: Use the system sans family, one action blue, semantic green/amber/red, and a simple bordered or subtly raised receipt. Remove continuous decoration and bounce; reserve one 150–250ms state transition for the result, with an instant reduced-motion path. Differentiate punctual, late-with-card, and late-with-penalty using honest headings and restrained semantic color.

personas.primary_flow_walkthrough: Alex opens attendance, identifies the active period, and taps a student; for point changes Alex filters/selects students, chooses reward/penalty, selects a preset, reviews the modal, and confirms.
red_flags: The attendance screen puts decorative and authoring content before the core grid, slowing a repeated classroom action.
There is no keyboard accelerator or search/group shortcut for attendance, and the points student grid cannot be selected by keyboard.
The reason rail hides its scrollbar and has no search/favorites/recent reasons, so expert speed degrades as presets grow.
Homework, running exercise, settings, and history lengthen the points page even when Alex only needs a quick adjustment.

personas.persona: Sam — keyboard, screen-reader, or reduced-motion user
personas.primary_flow_walkthrough: Sam tabs through navigation, selects a student, waits for a status announcement, then attempts the equivalent selection and confirmation in the points flow.
red_flags: Attendance student buttons are semantic, but the result overlay is not announced as status and does not manage focus or provide a dismiss action.
Points student tiles are click-only divs with no role, tab stop, or aria-pressed state, blocking the primary points path.
The custom modal has no role=dialog, aria-modal, focus trap, Escape handling, or accessible label on the close icon.
Infinite glow, pulse, firework, and quote animations have no reduced-motion alternative.
Delete buttons are icon-only and revealed mainly on hover, making them inaccessible or undiscoverable to touch and keyboard users.

personas.persona: Jordan — first-time student or substitute teacher
personas.primary_flow_walkthrough: Jordan arrives at the attendance screen, decides whether check-in is currently allowed, chooses a name, and tries to understand the resulting point consequence; Jordan then visits points to make a simple reward.
red_flags: The quote is visually more prominent than the instruction or current action, so the first tap is not obvious within five seconds.
Disabled student tiles do not explain locally when the next window begins; the rule chips require Jordan to connect distant information.
‘打卡成功’ is shown for late outcomes, forcing Jordan to reconcile celebration with a broken streak and deduction.
Teacher notice publishing, student messages, correction, and settings appear in the same experience without clear audience boundaries.
A points reason can be clicked before selecting students and only then produces a native alert, rather than guiding the expected order inline.

minor_observations: The attendance header gradient and indigo endpoint violate the design system’s single-action-blue rule.
The quotation’s Georgia typeface violates the one-family product rule and reduces cross-screen cohesion.
Several controls use px-3 py-1 and text-xs sizing; publish, correction, and maintenance actions need a 44px touch-target audit.
Teacher and student message inputs rely on placeholder text instead of persistent labels, and Enter handling uses onKeyPress rather than current keydown semantics.
The top navigation exposes eight items in one horizontal row; active state is clear, but maintenance and infrequent modules have equal prominence to attendance and points.
The success overlay cannot be paused or dismissed and its three-second timer is not friendly to readers with cognitive or low-vision needs.
The attendance quote calculation assumes quotes is a non-empty array; an empty configuration can yield undefined content or modulo-by-zero behavior.
Long student names and large rosters have no visible truncation, wrap, search, pagination, or group strategy on attendance.
The custom modal uses bounce motion even though product guidance explicitly prefers short state motion without bounce or elastic easing.
Points selection has useful check-icon plus border redundancy, but because the underlying element is non-semantic the accessible state is absent.

questions.question: If only one thing can dominate the classroom projection, should it be the daily quote, the current period, or the student’s result?
Should a late check-in ever use the same ‘🎉 打卡成功’ peak as punctual attendance?
options: No — use an honest warning receipt
Yes — celebrate participation but lower intensity
Only when a late card prevents deduction

questions.question: Are teacher notice publishing, student messaging, and maintenance intentionally exposed on the student-facing check-in screen?
What must remain visible after check-in for trust: exact point delta, new balance, correction path, or all three?

screenshots/visible evidence: Desktop dashboard at the default browser viewport after isolated-account login.

Authenticated attendance screen at 06:30 during the active 早读 period, showing the animated quote, active-session chip, teacher notice, today’s rules, 12-student grid, message board, and collapsed settings.

Authenticated points screen showing 12 click-style student tiles, selected-count toolbar, reward/penalty switch, 10 reason choices, collapsed settings, homework and running-exercise modules, and recent-history table.
browser.blocker: The live post-check-in result could not be captured: clicking the unique 张雨晴 attendance button unexpectedly returned the fresh tab to the login screen, produced no visible error, left no browser error/warning logs, and a read of the isolated attendance API showed no created record.
fallback: Used the authenticated pre-click attendance view as visual evidence of hierarchy, density, session states, and target sizing.

Traced the complete check-in handler and feedback rendering in public/attendance/module.js, including error codes, three-second timeout, status/point/streak copy, full-screen pointer-transparent overlay, and animation classes.
Used the authenticated points page plus public/operations/views.js, public/operations/actions.js, public/operations/module.js, and public/ui/modal.js for the adjacent operation-flow assessment.

cleanup.server: Isolated server ran in PTY session 4718 on 127.0.0.1:3211 and was stopped with Ctrl-C/SIGINT; the server reported graceful shutdown.
cleanup.temporary_account: assessmentteacher, created only in the temporary database
cleanup.browser_tab: Fresh agent-created browser tab finalized and closed; no existing tab was reused.
